children.text: The snares suggest connections to a long history of pistas and mixtaps which preceded the breakthrough pop smash and which remain as audible, palpable, if subtle remnants of an unbridled, underground, sample-based past in a genre that has since embraced slick synthesizers and commercial channels

Reggaetón is often described as a Puerto Rican innovation with origins traceable to the underground scene of San Juan during the 1990s. This narrative frames the genre as a product of artistic codemixing emerging from the simultaneous interplay of Afro-Caribbean musical influences resonating throughout Jamaica, Panama, Puerto Rico, and New York City. Below is an outline of this history with audio examples to help you follow along with the sonic development of one of the world's most widely consumed musical genres.
Step 1: The first 3 blocks are the primary musical influences.

"In New York, Panamanians, Jamaicans, Puerto Ricans, Dominicans, and African Americans all lived in proximity and were often racialized together. Therefore, genres like reggae and hip-hop resonanted powerfully as "Black musics" which, for all lines still drawn between groups, could embody and express new articulations of community" (Marshall, 2009 p. 33).

"Dancehall and hip-hop have tended to travel together, heard outside their principal sites of production as two sides of the same coin" (Marshall, 2009 p. 25). Dancehall made its way to San Juan not from Kingston, but from New York.
This direct link between Jamaica and Panama and the embrace of reggae produced a different ecology that than in Puerto Rico, to where the genre arrived indirectly as mediated by New York and hip-hop.

The predominant rhythmic orientation of reggaetón is derived directly from dancehall reggae. While there is also influence from roots reggae, dancehall's textures, tempos, rapid-fire flow, minimalist focus on 3+3+2 cross rhythms demarcated by heavy, synthesized drums, and local concerns with crime, drugs, violence, sex, poverty, and corruption have had a stronger stylistic influence. Much of dancehall's uptake in Panama and Puerto Rico was related to its counter-cultural connotations and close relationship with rap/hip-hop, as well as its capacity to circulate social semiotics of modernity, urbanity, and Blackness as political subjectivity. One of the most enduring features of this influence still audible in reggaetón today is the following
, a 4/4 kick-drum pulse with syncopated snares popularized in Shabba Ranks' song "Dem Bow" (1990).

children.caption: Skeleton of the Dem Bow Riddim from Marshall (2009)

children.text: With the growth and influence of New York's Jamaican community in the 1980s, by the 1990s dancehall had worked its way into the sets of hip-hop DJs and became a culturally charged feature of the New York soundscape. These riddims, such as the examples below, would later be incorporated into the underground recordings that laid the musical foundations for reggaetón.
While dancehall reggae gave the proto-reggaetón of the early 1990s its musical structure, the

of this period drew heavy stylistic influences from gangster rap and the "Latin rap" that diverged linguistically from the sonic template set by the former. Vico C is a foundational figure here, recognized as the father of
and the first to demonstrate how one could rap entirely and compellingly in Spanish (aside from the occassional insertion of hip-hop slang lyrics).
"I sing hip-hop on top of a reggaetón beat. I don't know how to write it any other way" (Tego Calderón, Marshall 2009 p. 27)

Prior to the global rise of reggae in the 1970s, Afro-Panamanian popular music was heavily based around the calypso performances of local favorites like Lord Cobra and Lord Panama. By the late 1970s, roots reggae had largely supplanted calypso, and from here, artists like El General, Nando Boom, and Chicho Man became early proponents of Panamanian reggae by performing some of the first Spanish-language reggae tracks. Since the 1980s, dancehall has dominated the scene through the consistent production of Spanish-language cover versions of contemporary dancehall hits replayed over the original Jamaican riddims. In this sense, Panamanian reggae exemplifies an emulation of dancehall rather than a derivative.
These remakes played a key role in shaping the dancehall scene in Puerto Rico and the Spanish-language dancehall scene in New York. Particularly influential is the Columbia Records production
(1991, prod. Karl Miller), which provided a template for layering Spanish-language lyrics over Jamaican riddims.

Dancehall and hip-hop were in close proximity during the 1990s in New York City, often heard outside their principal sites of production as two sides of the same coin. As a result, many aspiring rappers of Caribbean descent began transitioning to dancehall, framing their performances as rapping over baile-centric riddims that were more compatible with the sonic flavors of Caribbean culture. Following
, the Puerto Rican producer Jorge Oquendo alongside Vico C released the 8-track EP

(1991), a calculated attempt to develop a Latin pop/rap hybrid by bringing rising Puerto Rican rappers together with established merengue musicians. The track "Te Ves Buena Remix," —a sample-based, hip-hop-inflected remix of El General's dancehall track by the same name— is perhaps the closest track on this project to sound like the productions soon to emerge from the parallel, grassroots development of rap-reggae fusions in San Juan.

found their way to San Juan via Jorge Oquendo and DJ Negro, the latter who began operating The Noise nightclub in 1990. While the former played a marginal role in the development of reggaetón, the latter made more of a splash on the Island. But, without grassroots support, these productions were quickly supplanted by a similar sort of fusion which prioritized signifiers tied to the Black, urban, transnational sounds of hip-hop and dancehall. DJ Negro and DJ Playero played a pivotal role in the development of this new hybrid by merging the familiar melodic contours of Jamaican dancehall and hip-hop and recruiting emerging rappers like Baby Rasta, Daddy Yankee, Gringo, and Ivy Queen to rap over the tracks. These DJs built off practices of reuse and allusion, taking reggae's "riddim method" and hip-hop's sample-based collages to an unprecedented extreme to create a new hybrid genre—at times called

—was described as an intense fusion of rap with reggae that could not be clearly classified as one or the other. In these productions, Shabba Ranks' dem bow rhythmic pattern had been abstracted away from its original structure to become an important signifier that could be repurposed and combined with other elements to create distinctiveness within a system of resonant sonic signifiers.

content.caption: Basic Elements of Puerto Rico's localized Dem Bow

children.text: The underground recordings literally circulated outside the mainstream Puerto Rican economy and commercial channels, being dubbed from cassette to cassette from a small run of master tapes until about 1994. This underground distribution was in part due to the music's inherited reputation as the obscene music of the (racialized) underclass and clashes with the National Center on Sexual Exploitation (NCOSE, formerly Morality in Media) to keep them out of mainstream channels.
It wasn't until 1999 when DJ Nelson and DJ Coyote managed to get San Juan's mix 107.7 fm to begin a 24/7 reggaetón format station.
alternates the snares every four measures to create a subtle, stylistically grounded sense of movement against the otherwise repetitive structures of the track
